Looking at the choices: A) Time is equal to dollars, pounds... This seems too literal. The original proverb uses "money" metaphorically, not to say time is literally currency. The essay expands on this by saying time is more valuable than money, so if this option is suggesting a direct equivalence, it might not be correct. B) Time is gold. This is another common proverb, but the question is about what "Time is money" means. So this might be a distractor, as "Time is money" and "Time is gold" are both metaphors about valuing time, but the question is specifically about the given proverb. C) Time is valuable. This seems like the most straightforward interpretation. The proverb "Time is money" is used to emphasize the value of time, similar to how money is valuable. The essay supports this by saying time is more valuable than money, so the core idea is that time is valuable. D) We must spend time in buying goods. This seems off-topic.
